About

Engineer with a quantitative core

I am an engineer who likes to understand how a complex system performs, fails, and can be improved — and to back every conclusion with data and the maths behind it.

My foundation is a B.Sc. in Industrial Engineering (240 ECTS). Across the degree I worked on real, hands-on engineering — FEA and CFD simulations, mechanism kinematics, CAM/CNC manufacturing, structural and electrical design, maintenance management, and a thesis on the reliability of a production line. I also won 1st place in the Mirtale CubeSat CAD competition.

I am now extending that mindset into GNSS and satellite navigation through the JSNP master. The bridge is deliberate: the rigor I used for availability and reliability in industry maps onto GNSS performance — accuracy, integrity, availability and continuity.

I completed my engineering degree entirely alongside a full-time job in Germany — focused, reliable and persistent.

Mirtale Design Competition · 2024

CubeSat Structure — 1U Nanosatellite CAD Design

Problem

A national CAD competition: reproduce, with the maximum possible precision, the plan view of the cross-section of a 1U nanosatellite (CubeSat) bus — the structural “skeleton” that integrates the subsystems. It must withstand the launch vibrations of the rocket while staying light enough to ease the satellite’s orientation & navigation control. Judged on precision, clean dimensioning and the title block.

What I did

  • Modelled the monoblock frame with its 4-pin connectors fixed by through-bolts.
  • Fully dimensioned the part inside a standard title block, optimising cleanliness and drawing order.
  • Delivered the required package: dimensioned PDF, native DWG and a screenshot proving the use of ZWCAD.

Conclusion

1st place among all entries (prize: a ZWCAD licence). A tangible link to the space sector — satellite structures and the navigation subsystems I am now specializing in.

Skills: precision technical drawing · geometric dimensioning & tolerancing · ZWCAD/AutoCAD · aerospace structures awareness.

Bachelor Thesis · 2025–2026

System-Performance Optimization of a Packaging Line (Marchesini MA352)

Problem

On a high-speed pharma/cosmetic cartoning line (Marchesini MA352), the leaflet-insertion subsystem (GUK module) adds downtime, material and cost. The thesis asks: what is the real operational, material, environmental and economic impact of removing it?

Method

  • One year of anonymised plant data: OEE dashboard, time-loss log by category, production per SKU.
  • Pareto to prioritise downtime drivers and isolate the losses attributable to the subsystem.
  • A counterfactual “no-leaflet” scenario recomputing Availability & Performance, plus MTBF/MTTR reliability and a direct line-cost model.
ObservedOEE = Availability × Performance × Quality = 64.1% × 74.8% × 100% = 48.0%
ReliabilityMTBF = operating time / number of failures  ·  MTTR = repair time / failures

Conclusion

Leaflet-related losses = 7.6% of planned time (358 h/yr). Removing the subsystem gives +5.6 to +14 OEE points, avoids ≈ 67 t/yr of paper (50–90 t CO₂e) and removes a recurrent failure source from the line’s series reliability chain.

Skills: OEE/KPI · Pareto · MTBF/MTTR · counterfactual modelling · cost analysis · LCA basics · 85-page LaTeX report.

Fluid Mechanics · 2024

CFD & Similarity — Wind-Turbine Siting over Terrain

Problem

Find the best location for a wind turbine over a terrain with a step of height H, given incoming wind u = 12 m/s and H = 100 m. The real domain is ≈ 1.3 km long — too large to simulate directly, so a 1:100 scale model is used.

Method — similarity laws

To make the model represent the prototype I enforced geometric, kinematic and dynamic similarity. Equal Reynolds number (same fluid, air ρ = 1.196 kg/m³) gives the model velocity:

KinematicRem = ρ·A·U / μ = Rep  ⇒  Um = Ap·Up / Am = (500·12)/5 = 120 m/s
Dynamicρ·Am²·Pm/μ² = ρ·Ap²·Pp/μ² ⇒ Pm = 10⁴·Pp = 1.013×10⁸ Pa
Bernoulli + continuityP₁ + ½ρv₁² + ρgh₁ = P₂ + ½ρv₂² + ρgh₂  ;  A₁v₁ = A₂v₂

What I did

  • Built the geometry in SolidWorks and ran the CFD in SimScale with pressure inlet/outlet boundary conditions.
  • Post-processed the velocity field to locate the high-velocity zone and positioned the rotor there; tested a smooth low-friction ground variant.
  • Selected a real machine for the conditions: GE Vernova Haliade-X 12 MW.

Conclusion

The high-velocity band sits low, just over the step — the optimal rotor placement. The similarity + CFD workflow lets a small model drive a real siting and turbine-selection decision without a full-scale experiment.

Skills: CFD (SimScale) · dimensional analysis & similarity (Re) · Bernoulli/continuity · SolidWorks · turbine selection.

Machine Fundamentals · 2024

Mechanism Kinematics — Plier as a Four-Bar Linkage

Problem

A plier (pinza ABC/DEF): the upper handle & jaw are at rest, while the lower handle DEF rotates clockwise at a constant ω = 0.2 rad/s. Determine the angular acceleration of the lower jaw CFG — i.e. whether a constant input rotation induces acceleration elsewhere in the mechanism.

Method

The plier is modelled as a four-bar linkage and solved with relative-velocity vector kinematics: build the velocity of each point with cross products, then impose the fixed-point conditions to form and solve the equation system.

Relative velocityvB = vA + ω × rB/A
ConstraintFixed point F ⇒ vF = 0  ⇒  system of equations in the unknown angular velocities

Conclusion

Resolving the cross products and the fixed-point system yields the angular-velocity distribution and, from it, the angular acceleration of jaw CFG. The exercise shows how a constant input rotation propagates through a closed kinematic chain — the core skill behind mechanism and robotics analysis. Complemented with a GIM mechanism-simulation lab.

Skills: mechanism kinematics · vector mechanics (cross products, determinants) · four-bar linkages · GIM simulation.

Machine Fundamentals · 2024

Structural & Buckling Analysis of a Lattice High-Voltage Tower

Problem

A lattice high-voltage transmission tower must stay rigid and stable under combined service loads (conductor pull, wind). The task: verify its structural integrity and stability.

Method

  • Modelled the lattice tower in SolidWorks and ran static and buckling (Pandeo) FEA studies.
  • Evaluated reaction forces, member stresses and the stability margin under the load cases.
  • Cross-checked statics with Ftool and mechanism behaviour with GIM; documented in two technical lab reports.
Strength of materialsσ = F / A  (member stress)  ;  ε = (L’ − L) / L  (normal strain)
StabilityPcr = π²·E·I / (K·L)²  (Euler critical buckling load)

Conclusion

The static study returns the stress field and reactions; the buckling study gives the load factor before instability. Together they confirm whether the lattice geometry is both strong and stable — the standard verification for a transmission structure.

Skills: FEA (static & buckling) · SolidWorks Simulation · structural statics · Euler buckling · Ftool/GIM · technical reporting.

Industrial Manufacturing · 2025

CAM / CNC Manufacturing of a Machined Bracket (AISI 1020)

Problem

Manufacture a metal bracket by chip-removal machining: choose the material, define the full process and generate the CNC program.

Method & material

  • Designed the part in SolidWorks and selected AISI 1020 low-carbon steel for its machinability and availability.
  • Defined the operation sequence: stock cutting/roughing, milling (14 mm carbide 2-flute), drilling, tapping, finishing and grinding/polishing.
  • Chose tools and toolpaths per operation and generated the NC (CNC) code from the Mill/Turn operations.
MachiningVc = π·D·n / 1000  (cutting speed, m/min — D in mm, n in rpm)

Conclusion

A complete, manufacturable process: the AISI 1020 choice and the ordered roughing→finishing→grinding sequence deliver the required tolerance and surface finish, with a CNC program ready for the machine.

Skills: CAD (SolidWorks) · CAM & toolpath planning · CNC/NC-code · cutting parameters · materials & machinability.

Electrical Technology · 2024

Low-Voltage Electrical Installation (Residential)

Problem

Design the complete low-voltage electrical installation of a real dwelling, from the grid connection to the final circuits, with proper schematics and protection.

Method

  • Drew the distribution network: transmission/distribution substation → service connection (acometida) → general protection box (CGP) → individual derivation.
  • Produced single-line and circuit schematics in SolidWorks Electrical plus the CAD floor plan (DWG) of the home.
  • Sized and specified the protections — meter, fuses and breakers — according to regulation.
SizingI = P / (√3 · U · cos φ)  (three-phase line current → conductor & breaker sizing)

Conclusion

A coherent, regulation-compliant installation that ties together electrical calculation, single-line logic and technical drawing — the multidisciplinary work behind a real building’s electrical project.

Skills: electrical design · single-line diagrams · SolidWorks Electrical · AutoCAD/DWG · protection & load sizing · regulations.

Maintenance Engineering · 2025

Industrial Maintenance Plan — Safety-Helmet Plant

Problem

Design a maintenance plan for a safety-helmet manufacturing plant (plastic injection moulding + assembly + QC) that maximises equipment availability while controlling cost — focusing effort where it matters most.

Method

  • Mapped the manufacturing process and built the equipment inventory (injection machines, Piovan hopper, pneumatic conveyor, packing/labelling…).
  • Criticality analysis to rank every asset, then a maintenance strategy by criticality level.
  • Annual preventive plan with a dated checklist, responsibilities, per-equipment sheets, failure analysis and a 5S / Kaizen / TPM culture layer.
PrioritisationCriticality = Production Impact × Failure Probability  →  High / Medium / Low
KPIsMTBF = total operating time / number of failures  ;  Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)

Conclusion

The criticality ranking concentrates preventive effort on the equipment whose failure most hurts production, while low-criticality assets run to corrective. The result is a defensible, KPI-driven plan — the same logic a real plant uses to balance availability and cost.

Skills: preventive maintenance planning · criticality analysis · maintenance KPIs (MTBF/MTTR/availability) · TPM · 5S/Kaizen.

Maintenance Engineering · 2026

Maintenance Digitalisation with a CMMS (ManWinWin)

Problem

Take the maintenance plan from paper to a real CMMS/GMAO platform so it can actually be managed, tracked and improved.

What I did

  • Built the functional asset hierarchy (organisation → systems → sub-systems) of the plant inside ManWinWin.
  • Created and managed work orders and separated preventive vs corrective strategy.
  • Tracked reliability/maintenance KPIs to support decisions.

Conclusion

A working digital backbone for maintenance: structured assets, traceable work orders and KPIs — turning the plan into something a maintenance team can run day-to-day.

Skills: CMMS/GMAO (ManWinWin) · asset structuring · work-order management · preventive maintenance · reliability KPIs.

Product Design · 2025

Solar Collector — Design & Structural Verification

Problem

Develop a solar (air) collector as a product — from concept to a verified, manufacturable assembly — under sustainability constraints.

Method

  • Designed the collector and its housing as a full SolidWorks assembly (base, casing, components) with STEP export.
  • Ran a static FEA to verify the structure under load.
  • Framed the work with Design Thinking and concurrent-engineering / eco-design principles (sustainable materials, prevention at source).

Conclusion

A coherent product concept that pairs CAD design with structural verification and an explicit sustainability lens — design that is buildable, not just drawable.

Skills: SolidWorks assemblies · static FEA · Design Thinking · concurrent engineering · sustainable design.

Operations Research · 2025

Optimization & Operations Research

Problem

Make optimal production and design decisions: minimise cost / maximise revenue and profit in a factory, and design packaging that uses the least material.

Method

  • Formulated the problems as linear programs (objective + constraints) and solved them graphically and with Solver.
  • For the packaging, used calculus optimization: minimise surface area subject to a fixed volume (critical points).
  • Also covered transportation and assignment problems (Hungarian method) in the wider course.
LPmax Z = revenue − cost   s.t.   resource & demand constraints
Packagingmin S(x,y,z) = surface area   s.t.   V = x·y·z = constant

Conclusion

Translating a business decision into an objective function with constraints turns intuition into a defensible optimum — the quantitative backbone of operations and logistics.

Skills: linear programming · constrained optimization · transportation/assignment models · Excel Solver · decision modelling.
